failure_modes.text: Temporary coexistence can quietly become the architecture that traps the organization in both the old and new path.
failure_modes.source_quote: The high-risk pattern is "temporary coexistence" becoming the default architecture.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set a coexistence deadline: define when the old system will be fully retired and track dual-run dependencies to prevent temporary parallel operation from becoming permanent.

failure_modes.text: Teams can assume rollback stays available even while dual-run dependencies and customizations are rapidly making reversal harder.
failure_modes.source_quote: The result is not just delayed efficiency; it is accumulated irreversibility.
failure_modes.mitigation: Monitor rollback feasibility continuously: at each milestone, re-verify that reversal is still possible and flag the moment when customizations or dependencies make it irreversible.

failure_modes.text: A familiar framework can get defended beyond fit because the mental or organizational cost of switching feels too high.
failure_modes.source_quote: we are uncomfortable with our inconsistencies and will take steps to reduce or remove them
failure_modes.mitigation: Evaluate framework loyalty against current fit: periodically check whether the incumbent tool still serves the problem better than alternatives, stripping out familiarity comfort from the comparison.

premortem_questions.text: What specific capability, workflow, or partner dependency would force both the old and new path to stay alive longer than planned?
premortem_questions.source_quote: what specific capabilities must remain alive during migration

premortem_questions.text: How fast does the cost of going back rise as data history, customizations, and contracts accumulate under the new state?
premortem_questions.source_quote: how fast does the cost of going back increase as data history, customizations, and partner contracts accumulate

premortem_questions.text: What foundational assumptions are embedded in the framework we are currently reluctant to switch away from?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What foundational assumptions are embedded in the primary framework we are using, and are those assumptions guaranteed to hold true?

heuristics.text: Treat dual-run coexistence as a real coordination tax instead of as harmless implementation detail.
heuristics.source_quote: The real economic and operational burden comes from keeping both the old and new path alive at the same time.

heuristics.text: Lower mental switching cost for a good new idea by using analogies that connect it to something the audience already understands.
heuristics.source_quote: Analogies act as "bridges, connecting what your audience already understands to the new ideas".

heuristics.text: Tie exit sequencing to named owners and checkpoints so reversibility decay becomes visible before it is too late.
heuristics.source_quote: exit sequencing that is not tied to named owners, checkpoints, and decision gates leaves the organization without a trigger to stop and re-plan

curation_notes.summary: The source is strongest on coexistence drag, reversibility decay, and cognitive entrenchment rather than on generic customer-retention rhetoric.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor vendor-migration overlays beyond the raw source's clearer dual-run, reversal-cost, and anti-entrenchment material.
open_questions: The third failure mode generalizes the source's commitment-consistency thread so it stays useful beyond infrastructure exits.

allies: The raw allies section is strong on cognitive-load-theory, framing, and anchoring as forces that reinforce incumbent paths.
antagonists: The raw antagonists section is strongest on intellectual-humility, first-principles-thinking, and feynman-technique.
structured_tensions: The source contains an explicit structured tension block naming optionality, feedback-loops, and confirmation-bias.
